FT_IC1.evidence: Longitudinal data from 10,317 participants in U.S. ABCD Study, baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-10 years, analyzed through Year 2

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; logistic regression analyses conducted to estimate associations between ACEs and cyberbullying victimization

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of Adolescence

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper examines ACEs (traumatic life events) as predictor and cyberbullying victimization as outcome. Cyberbullying alone without separately-measured smartphone/SM variable does not qualify. No qualifying SM/phone variable as IV or DV paired with individual-trait-level variable.

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC2 (no qualifying smartphone/SM variable)

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from Year 2 of U.S. ABCD Study, 2018-2020, ages 10-14 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; modified Poisson regression analyses examined associations between cyberbullying and sleep disturbance

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Academic Pediatrics

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper examines cyberbullying victimization and perpetration as predictors and sleep disturbance as outcome. Cyberbullying alone without separately-measured smartphone/SM variable does not qualify. No qualifying SM/phone variable as IV or DV paired with individual-trait-level variable.

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC2 (cyberbullying-only, no qualifying SM/phone variable)

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from Year 2 of U.S. ABCD Study, N=10,339, 2018-2020, ages 10-14 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; multiple linear regression analyses estimated associations between sexual orientation and recreational screen time and problematic screen use

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Annals of Epidemiology

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone/SM variables as DV: screen time (TV, YouTube, video games, texting, social media, video chat, browsing internet); sexual orientation as IV (individual-trait-level). Methods state 'recreational screen use for the following modalities: multi-player gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing the internet, and watching/streaming movies, videos, or TV.' Results report screen time associations across multiple modalities including social media and phone-based activities (texting, video chat).

FT_IC3.evidence: Inferential analysis with quantitative results: 'sexual minority (compared to heterosexual) identification was associated with 3.72 (95% CI 2.96-4.47) more hours of daily recreational screen time, specifically more time on television, YouTube videos, video games, texting, social media, video chat, and browsing the internet.' Regression beta coefficients reported for each modality.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric results reported: sexual minority vs. heterosexual differences in screen time (3.72 hours; 95% CI 2.96-4.47); individual modality differences (TV 0.51 hrs, YouTube 0.94 hrs, games 0.36-0.57 hrs, texting 0.64 hrs, social media 0.46 hrs, video chat 0.24 hrs, browsing 0.52 hrs). Problematic use scores reported for video games (0.49 higher), social media (0.46 higher), and mobile phone (0.56 higher).

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional; EC7 applies only to longitudinal studies with SM-as-DV-only. This paper has SM as DV but design is not longitudinal.
notes: INCLUDE: Meets all FT-IC criteria and EC7 does not apply.
